title: "Amber Labyrinth Puzzle v11429"

description: |
  At 22 : 05 the Amber Labyrinth Orb vanished from its locked pedestal.
  Four badge-holders (1 2 3 4) were logged nearby.
  Exactly one suspect lies in every statement they utter; the other three speak only truths.
  Exactly one suspect is guilty.
  The universal liar and the thief are different people.

  Suspect 1
    (1) “If 2 is the universal liar, then 3 is definitely not the thief.”
    (2) “If 4 was at the pedestal at 22 : 05, then 2 cannot be the liar.”
    (3) “Statement 3-(2) is itself false.”
    (4) “I am certainly not the thief.”

  Suspect 2
    (1) “I am the thief.”
    (2) “I was at the pedestal at 22 : 05.”
    (3) “Every line uttered by 3 is a lie.”
    (4) “If 4 really was present, then 1 cannot be the liar.”

  Suspect 3
    (1) “Every word of 2 is false.”
    (2) “4 was nowhere near the pedestal at 22 : 05.”
    (3) “Whether I saw 2 at 22 : 05 matches exactly whether 4 is the liar.”
    (4) “If 2 is the thief, then 4 is the liar.”

  Suspect 4
    (1) “I am not the thief.”
    (2) “I was indeed present at 22 : 05.”
    (3) “1 is the liar iff 3 is the thief.”
    (4) “Should 1 turn out to be the liar, that forces 2 to be the thief.”

question: "Who stole the Amber Labyrinth Orb? Return a single digit 1–4."

answer: >
  explanation: |
    Assume $L1$.

    $1$–$(3)$ is $\lnot S_{3,2}$, which is true if $S_{3,2}$ is false.  
    But $S_{3,2}$ is the truth value of $3$–$(2)$, which is currently undetermined.  
    So $1$–$(3)$ is not guaranteed false.  
    Therefore $L1$ is invalid.

    Assume $L2$.

    $2$–$(1)$ false $\implies G2$ is false  
    $2$–$(2)$ false $\implies P2$ is false  
    $2$–$(3)$ false $\implies L3$ is false  
    $2$–$(4)$ false $\implies P4$ is true and $L1$ is true

    This forces two liars: $L1$ and $L2$  
    So $L2$ is invalid

    Assume $L3$.

    $3$–$(1)$ false $\implies L2$ is false  
    $3$–$(2)$ false $\implies P4$ is true  
    $3$–$(3)$ false means $P2 \leftrightarrow L4$ is false  
    Set $P2$ true, $L4$ false  
    $3$–$(4)$ false $\implies G2$ is true, $L4$ is false

    Current assignment:  
    $L3 = \text{true}$  
    $G2 = \text{true}$  
    $P2 = \text{true}$  
    $P4 = \text{true}$  
    $L1 = L2 = L4 = \text{false}$  
    $G1 = G3 = G4 = \text{false}$

    Check suspect $2$:

    $2$–$(1)$ is $G2$ true  
    $2$–$(2)$ is $P2$ true  
    $2$–$(3)$ is $L3$ true  
    $2$–$(4)$ is $P4 \implies \lnot L1$, both true

    Check suspect $1$:

    $1$–$(1)$ is $L2 \implies \lnot G3$, $L2$ false $\implies$ true  
    $1$–$(2)$ is $P4 \implies \lnot L2$, both true  
    $1$–$(3)$ is $\lnot S_{3,2}$  
    $3$–$(2)$ is false, so $S_{3,2}$ is false $\implies$ $\lnot S_{3,2}$ is true  
    $1$–$(4)$ is $\lnot G1$, which is true

    Check suspect $4$:

    $4$–$(1)$ is $\lnot G4$, which is true  
    $4$–$(2)$ is $P4$, which is true  
    $4$–$(3)$ is $L1 \leftrightarrow G3$, both false $\implies$ true  
    $4$–$(4)$ is $L1 \implies G2$, antecedent false $\implies$ true

    All suspects except $3$ are truthful  
    Exactly one liar, exactly one thief, and they are different

  final: '2'
